title: Quieting Food Noise After GLP-1 Discontinuation: A Feasibility Study of a Novel Mindful Eating Program
keywords: assessments at baseline/12 weeks/3 months/6 months

summary.hook: This poster proposes and tests the feasibility of a 12-week online mindful eating program as a structured “off-ramp” for adults who discontinue GLP-1 receptor agonist therapy.
summary.summary_30s: This poster proposes and tests the feasibility of a 12-week online mindful eating program as a structured “off-ramp” for adults who discontinue GLP-1 receptor agonist therapy. The rationale is that GLP-1s reduce appetite and “food noise” during use, but discontinuation often leads to renewed food noise, dysregulated eating, and weight regain—creating “behavioral chaos” without a behavioral plan. The study is a randomized, controlled feasibility trial (intervention vs wait-list control with crossover), measuring feasibility (recruitment, retention, adherence, satisfaction) and preliminary efficacy outcomes including food noise (RAID-FN), eating-related distress, anxiety/depression symptoms, and quality of life, with follow-ups at 3 and 6 months.
talktrack_2m: Focus: Evaluate feasibility (recruitment, retention, adherence, satisfaction) of a 12-week online mindful eating program for adults discontinuing GLP-1 therapy.

Approach: Population: Adults who discontinued GLP-1 receptor agonist therapy within 2–3 weeks (no diabetes; no active eating disorder; interested in improving relationship with food). Primary outcome: Feasibility outcomes—recruitment rate, retention rate, session attendance/adherence, participant satisfaction.
Finding: This poster is primarily a protocol/rationale poster; empirical outcome results are Not stated on poster.

Why it matters: Authors’ stated conclusions: There are currently no evidence-based behavioral interventions designed as a clinically meaningful off-ramp following GLP-1 therapy; the presented mindful eating program is intended to provide structured support during discontinuation and inform future interventions reducing return to pre-medication behaviors. Implications (poster-grounded): Clinicians may need a “transition plan” for patients discontinuing GLP-1s because discontinuation is common and often followed by return of food noise, dysregulated eating, and weight regain. A scalable, skills-based online group program could fill an identified clinical gap during GLP-1 discontinuation by targeting upstream cognitive/attentional mechanisms (food noise) linked to eating behavior. Mechanism/explanation (ONLY if stated): GLP-1s quiet physiology (reduce appetite signaling, dampen reward salience, reduce intrusive food thoughts), but do not build durable self-regulation skills; mindful eating strengthens top-down processes (attentional control, interoceptive awareness, flexible decision-making) needed after pharmacologic support is withdrawn. Connections to prior work (ONLY if referenced): Poster includes eight references (KFF poll; JAMA Network Open discontinuation/reinitiation; STEP 1 extension; BMJ meta-analysis; Nutrients conceptual model; Cureus neurocircuit framing; Am I Hungry?® book; RAID-FN validation).
key_numbers: * Intervention duration: 12 weeks (12 weekly sessions; 90 minutes each).
Adults who discontinued GLP-1 receptor agonist therapy within 2–3 weeks (no diabetes; no active eating disorder; interested in improving relationship with food).
Poster reports background statistics: 12% of adults have taken a GLP-1; 54% discontinue by 1 year; 72% within 2 years; 46–57% restart within 2 years; after stopping semaglutide,...
This poster proposes and tests the feasibility of a 12-week online mindful eating program as a structured “off-ramp” for adults who discontinue GLP-1 receptor agonist therapy.
The study is a randomized, controlled feasibility trial (intervention vs wait-list control with crossover), measuring feasibility (recruitment, retention, adherence, satisfactio...
takeaways: This poster is primarily a protocol/rationale poster; empirical outcome results are Not stated on poster.
The poster provides background statistics suggesting high GLP-1 uptake, high discontinuation, frequent re-initiation, and substantial weight regain after stopping semaglutide.

The poster proposes a conceptual model (“Chaos → Calm Adaptive Pathway Model”) in which discontinuation creates a vulnerability window; mindful eating is framed as building durable “top-down” self-regulation that complements pharmacologic “bottom-up” appetite suppression.
limitations: The poster does not list formal study limitations.
The poster leaves open this clarification: What feasibility benchmarks define success (minimum recruitment/retention/adherence/satisfaction thresholds)?

what_to_ask.question: The poster cites 54% discontinuation by 1 year and 72% within 2 years—how does your study population compare to those broader user patterns?
what_to_ask.why_this_is_good: This gets the presenter to interpret the main result instead of repeating the title.

what_to_ask.question: What strategies will you use to handle missing data across baseline, 12-week, 3-month, and 6-month assessments?
what_to_ask.why_this_is_good: This probes confidence, assumptions, or limitations without sounding hostile.

what_to_ask.question: How could clinicians operationalize an “off-ramp” plan in routine care—when should it start relative to stopping GLP-1s?
what_to_ask.why_this_is_good: This opens a useful conversation about why the work matters or where it goes next.

collaboration_ideas.idea: If feasibility is supported, what would your next-step efficacy trial look like (sample size, endpoints, longer follow-up)
collaboration_ideas.ideal_partner: Partner aligned with the poster's next-step question
collaboration_ideas.what_they_contribute: Access, implementation context, data, or methods needed for the next phase named on the poster.
collaboration_ideas.what_they_get: A concrete role in shaping and evaluating the poster's next step.
